meta.name: Azure AI Fundamentals (AI-900)
meta.description: 10 foundational Azure AI and machine learning concepts for the AI-900 certification exam.

cards.definition: A subset of AI where systems learn from data to make predictions or decisions without being explicitly programmed. Azure Machine Learning is the managed service for building, training, and deploying ML models. Key types: Supervised (labelled data), Unsupervised (unlabelled data), and Reinforcement Learning (reward-based).
cards.hint: The system learns patterns from examples rather than following hand-coded rules.

cards.definition: A collection of pre-built AI APIs and models that developers can add to applications without ML expertise. Organised into five pillars: Vision (image analysis, OCR), Speech (speech-to-text, text-to-speech), Language (NLP, translation, sentiment), Decision (personalisation, anomaly detection), and OpenAI (GPT, DALL-E). Consumed via REST API or SDKs.
cards.hint: Ready-made AI brains you plug into your app via an API call.

cards.definition: An AI capability that enables systems to interpret and understand visual information from images and videos. Azure Computer Vision can describe images, detect objects and faces, read text (OCR), and classify content. Azure Custom Vision lets you train your own image classifiers and object detectors with your own labelled images.
cards.hint: Teaching machines to see — identifying what's in an image.

cards.definition: An AI field that enables computers to understand, interpret, and generate human language. Azure AI Language services include: Sentiment Analysis (positive/negative tone), Key Phrase Extraction, Named Entity Recognition (people, places), Language Detection, and Question Answering. Azure Translator provides real-time text translation across 100+ languages.
cards.hint: Teaching machines to read and understand text the way humans do.

cards.definition: A managed service for building, testing, and deploying conversational AI bots. Bots can be integrated with channels like Microsoft Teams, websites, and Slack. Built on the Bot Framework SDK and often uses Azure AI Language's Question Answering or CLU (Conversational Language Understanding) to interpret user intent and respond intelligently.
cards.hint: The Azure platform for building chatbots that understand and respond to natural language.

cards.definition: Microsoft's six principles for developing AI ethically: Fairness (no bias across groups), Reliability and Safety (consistent, tested performance), Privacy and Security (data protection), Inclusiveness (accessible to everyone), Transparency (explainable decisions), and Accountability (humans remain responsible). These underpin all Azure AI services and are a core AI-900 exam topic.
cards.hint: FRPITA: Fairness, Reliability, Privacy, Inclusiveness, Transparency, Accountability.

cards.definition: A web-based IDE within Azure Machine Learning for building ML workflows. Offers three authoring experiences: Automated ML (AutoML) — automatically tries algorithms and hyperparameters; Designer — drag-and-drop pipeline builder; and Notebooks — full Python/R coding environment. All three produce trained models that can be deployed as real-time or batch inference endpoints.
cards.hint: The no-code, low-code, and pro-code workbench for ML in Azure.

cards.definition: An AI technique that identifies data points or patterns that deviate significantly from the expected norm. Azure Anomaly Detector API analyses time-series data (e.g., website traffic, sensor readings) and flags unexpected spikes, dips, or trend changes — without needing labelled examples of anomalies. Commonly used for fraud detection, predictive maintenance, and monitoring.
cards.hint: Teaching the system to spot the odd one out in a stream of data over time.

cards.definition: A managed Azure service providing access to OpenAI's powerful language and image models — including GPT-4, GPT-3.5, Codex, and DALL-E — with enterprise-grade security, compliance, and regional data residency. Enables developers to build generative AI applications (copilots, content generation, code assistants) while keeping data within Azure's compliance boundary.
cards.hint: OpenAI's GPT and DALL-E models, but secured and hosted inside Azure.

cards.definition: Three fundamental ML task types: Regression predicts a continuous numeric value (e.g., house price, temperature). Classification assigns input to a discrete category (e.g., spam/not spam, disease/no disease). Clustering groups unlabelled data into natural segments based on similarity — no predefined labels needed. Regression and Classification are supervised; Clustering is unsupervised.
cards.hint: Regression = a number. Classification = a category. Clustering = find groups on your own.
